attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-week history of an moderately itchy rash on her torso and neck. The rash began as red bumps over her back and chest which have now turned brown. She had laparoscopic gastric bypass two years ago but has put on weight since then. She reports following a strict ketogenic diet for the past two months for weight loss and has lost 5 kilograms as a result.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her BMI is 38 kg/m2 with central obesity. There is a symmetrical reticulated pattern of brown macules on her upper back, chest and neck, with some overlying erythematous papules, sparing her face. The rash is not tender.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of prurigo pigmentosa: recurrent erythematous papules that resolve leaving reticulated hyperpigmentation triggered by the ketogenic diet. The condition has a strong association with ketosis, particularly in the setting of ketogenic diets, fasting, or eating disorders. The distribution on the back, chest, and neck is typical.

Prurigo pigmentosa is more common in young women of Asian descent, and is characterised by its distinct clinical course of inflammatory papules followed by reticulated hyperpigmentation. The association with ketosis is well-documented, and can also be associated with bariatric surgery and diabetes. Treatment of prurigo pigmentosa typically involves doxycycline or minocycline for their anti-inflammatory properties, along with addressing the underlying ketosis. Symptoms improve with increasing carbohydrate consumption though the pigmentation takes time to self-resolve.

Allergic contact dermatitis typically presents as pruritic, erythematous patches or plaques in areas of contact with an allergen. While it can be widespread and mildly itchy, it would not present with the characteristic reticulated pattern seen in this case, and there is usually a clear history of exposure to a triggering substance.

Acanthosis nigricans presents as dark, velvety, thickened plaques in flexural areas, particularly the neck and axillae. It is associated with insulin resistance and obesity rather than ketosis, and the rash is different to the patient's presentation.

Macular amyloidosis presents with pruritic, hyperpigmented, rippled patches typically on the upper back. While it can be pruritic, it lacks the initial inflammatory phase and has no association with ketosis.

Postinflammatory hyperpigmentation occurs following inflammation or injury to the skin. While it can appear similar, it requires a preceding inflammatory condition and would not present with active inflammatory papules alongside the hyperpigmentation.
learningPoints: Prurigo pigmentosa is a rare inflammatory skin condition characterised by recurrent erythematous papules that heal with reticulated hyperpigmentation.
Prurigo pigmentosa is strongly associated with ketosis, particularly in the setting of ketogenic diets or eating disorders.
Doxycycline is the first-line treatment for prurigo pigmentosa due to its anti-inflammatory properties.
references: Chen P, Oakley A. Prurigo pigmentosa. DermNet NZ. 2020.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/prurigo-pigmentosa

Aerts O, Dendooven E, Siozopoulou V. Dieting Resulting in Prurigo Pigmentosa (“Keto Rash”). The Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology in Practice. 2021: 9 (11).  Available at: https://www.jaci-inpractice.org/article/S2213-2198(21)00883-7/abstract
